title: 2024 Adobe Illustrator Tips and Tricks: Resolving Design Challenges
description:
In this video, you'll uncover a treasure trove of Adobe Illustrator tips and tricks tailored for 2024. Join me as I share my insights and knowledge gained over the years to help you tackle design challenges more efficiently.

1. Remove unwanted holes in your design effortlessly.
2. Streamline messy corners for a cleaner look.
3. Achieve perfectly rounded corners with ease.
4. Master the 'Minus Front' Pathfinder challenge.
5. Edit text precisely with the Touch Type Tool.
6. Create smooth zigzag paths quickly.
7. Move shapes pixel by pixel for precision.
8. Cut intricate shapes with ease.
9. Use a shortcut to spot transparency.
10. Fix gray color fill issues effortlessly.

#illustratortips
transcript: In this video, I'll be sharing some tips and tricks for Adobe Illustrator that I've learned  over the years. If you have a hole in your design that you want to remove, an easy way  is to select any point within that hole using the direct selection tool and press the delete key  twice. If you only press it once, you might be left with some extra straight points, so make sure  to press it twice. You can also select multiple points and holes at once and perform the same  action to delete all those points. Sometimes you might encounter designs with imperfect corners,  as you can see here. They often have numerous points and fixing them individually can be time  consuming. Here's a more efficient method. Identify a point just before the messier area begins.  Intersect it with another segment to create a cleaner design. Select the resulting shape and  use the Unite function. In the Pathfinder tool, this will remove the extra bits. You can then  select them and press Delete twice, as mentioned in the previous tip.  Now you have clear corner and you can even round it if necessary. This technique also applies to  interior corners. However, sometimes it leaves tiny holes, so remember to delete those as well.  The next tip relates to something that has happened to me when trying to round the corners of a  design. Using the Direct Selection tool, you can see that this leaf rounds nicely. However,  when attempting the same for another one, I encounter a problem. The corner remains straight  instead of becoming round. The solution I've found for this issue is to initially move in the  opposite direction until it reaches a point and then proceed in the desired direction. This will  result in a rounded corner as it should be. Let's say you have a design from which you want to cut  out a piece. For example, you want to make the bottom part straight by drawing a rectangle.  You select both the design and the rectangle and then use the minus front option in the Pathfinder  tool to remove that part. However, what just happened? You're left with only an eye? This is a  common issue for beginners, so let's undo that. The reason this happens is that we attempted to  remove a shape from a group. As you can see, this monkey consists of multiple shapes within the  group. To fix this, you can select the group and go to Object then Compound Path and choose Make.  This will create a single shape from the group. Now, when you repeat the same steps, it works as  it should. You can see it has created a new group with all the elements. Let's undo and show you  a different method. Even if it's a group, you can use the Direct Selection tool to select the  rectangle and the specific shape you want to cut, even if it's within the group. What this does  is remove that particular shape from the group during the operation. If you have text and want to  move or scale a specific letter like X without converting the text to outlines to keep it editable,  there's a solution. Under the Type tool, you'll find the Touch Type tool. It instructs you to  click on a character, so I'll click on X. Now I can scale it, rotate it, position it as I want,  or even move it. This tool provides a high level of control while keeping the text editable.  And you can also adjust the spacing between two letters by clicking between them and pressing Alt  plus the right or left arrow key. This allows you to fine tune the kerning for a pleasing appearance.  As you can see, even if I change the font, I can maintain the same arrangement and edits.  If you want to create a smooth zigzag path with the pen tool, it can be quite challenging to  maintain consistency. However, there's an alternative approach. Start by creating a straight line.  Go to Effects, then choose Distort and Transform and select Zigzag. Here you'll find various options  including size, the number of ridges per segment, and the ability to convert it to smooth  points. Experiment with the sliders until you achieve the desired effect. This technique isn't  limited to straight lines. It can also be applied to other shapes. For instance, if you create a  circle and apply the same effects, you can easily create various badge-like shapes. This method can  be quite useful and time-saving. When you need to move shapes around with precision, using the mouse  may not provide the accuracy you require. I prefer to use the arrow keys for precise movement,  but by default, it might move too far compared to what I want. To address this,  follow these steps. Go to Edit, then Preferences, and select General. You'll find a field labeled  Keyboard Increment. Enter 1px into this field and press Enter. Now, when you use the arrow keys,  the shapes will move only one pixel at a time, making it much easier to achieve precise control  and arrange your shapes exactly how you want them. I have here a UFO attempting to abduct a cow,  and I'm drawing a shape with the pen tool to represent the abduction beam. Now, I want to cut the  shape of the cow from the beam. Here are the steps. Select the shape of the cow and ensure it's  one single shape. If not, make it a compound path. Go to Object, then Path, and choose Offset Path.  This allows you to offset the path with both positive and negative values.  If needed, you can fine-tune it by using the Up and Down arrow keys. You can also change the  joints, making them round if desired. Select the newly created path and then select the UFO beam.  In the Pathfinder panel, depending on the layer order, you can choose either Minus Front,  or if that's not the right one. Use Undo and select Minus Back. This results in a nice contour  where the cow shape has been cut out. You can undo and move the shapes around before making the cut.  For better visibility, you can go to View, select Outline, and see each path clearly.  Once you're ready, return to Preview,  and repeat the Pathfinder operation. It looks great.  If desired, you can repeat the Offset Path on the UFO itself and cut that shape from the beam,  deleting any extra shapes as needed.  Sometimes it can be challenging to determine whether a design has a background or is transparent.  However, there's a handy shortcut to help you with this. Press Control plus Shift plus D,  and it will bring up the transparency grid. This makes it easy to check what parts of your design  are transparent and what parts are not. I have a document with a gray rectangle.  When I select it and choose a green fill color instead of gray, then press OK.  The result is still a gray rectangle. What the fill? Let's try another color. Maybe it doesn't like  greens as it's not vegetable friendly. This time I get a darker gray. Now, let's create a new  rectangle and make it red. Attempting to change the color of the first one again, still gray.  So how can you fix this problem? Here's the solution. Go to Select, then all to choose all  objects in the document. Next, navigate to Edit and select Edit Colors. Choose either convert to  CMYK or convert to RGB depending on your specific needs. Now, if we try to change the color,  it works as it should. Let me know in the comments which tip was your favorite. Have a great day.
